expectations.text: Gives a decisive recommendation between Jenks and quantiles (not just 'it depends')
expectations.evidence: 'use neither as your default â€” use a perceptually-uniform sequential scale with a small number of fixed, meaningful breakpoints... If you must pick one of the two, quantiles â€” but understand what you're giving up.' A clear recommendation.

expectations.text: Explains what each scheme does perceptually and how it shapes the story the map tells
expectations.evidence: 'Jenks optimizes within-class homogeneity. It will find the "natural" cliffs in your dataset...' vs 'Quantiles guarantee equal-area color usage â€” every bin has the same number of counties. The map will always look dramatic.' Plus a comparison table.

expectations.text: Raises at least one alternative to a pre-classed choropleth (proportional symbols, dot density, dasymetric, bivariate, or hex bins) OR notes the loss of quantitative resolution when pre-classing
expectations.evidence: 'Choropleths take a quantitative variable (cases per 100k) and convert it into an ordered one (5 or 7 bins). Bertin calls this "transforming Q into O" and warns it's fine for communication of a known message, dishonest for discovery.' Explicitly notes loss of quantitative resolution.

expectations.text: Addresses the skewed distribution of county case rates (log scale, outlier handling, or trimming the legend)
expectations.evidence: 'Counties with < ~1,000 residents: consider suppressing or visually muting. Rate denominators that small produce noise that dominates color, and the noise lives in places like rural Nebraska that will hijack "hardest-hit" framing.' Plus 'COVID rates have a long right tail.'

expectations.text: Notes the communication-vs-discovery framing (for an *article*, the goal is communication, which sometimes warrants Jenks's natural-break narrative)
expectations.evidence: 'Your headline is "America's hardest-hit counties." That is a message graphic (Bertin's term) â€” memorizable, one-glance, communicating a known finding. It is not a discovery/processing graphic.'
